key_insight: Exciton transport in perovskite nanocrystal solids is governed more by energetic disorder than by size/shape polydispersity, and longer alkylamine ligands can reduce transport efficiency despite making the nanocrystals more uniform.
engineering_relevance: Ligand length is a key design knob for tuning nanoscale order and exciton migration in lead halide perovskite films, with direct implications for LEDs, lasers, and solar cells.
method: Resolved exciton dynamics in space, time, and energy
Compared nanocrystal solids synthesized with different alkylamine ligand lengths
Separated the effects of structural disorder from energetic disorder on transport
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that longer-chain ligands produced more monodispersed, smaller nanocrystals but also stronger energetic disorder and less efficient exciton transport.
watch_item: The conclusions are specific to solution-processed disordered perovskite nanocrystal solids, so transport trends may shift with different compositions, ligands, or film-assembly conditions.
paper_title: Exciton Transport in Disordered Perovskite Nanocrystal Solids
